titleStmt.title: Morale and Home Intelligence Reports
Report: Morale Report on FloydForce, 8 Dec 44

div.note: Ref your letter LFA/44/AG 21 Nov herewith Morale Report.
111 Fd Regt RA 'A' Comp Sec 1556 Arty
180/64 HAA Bty RA Det 150 Pnr Coy
Tp 101/31 LAA Bty RA 37 mil Mission
Mov & Tn 2825 Fd Sqn RAP Regt
Det 2 SS Bde Sigs 514 Coy RASC
Bet 54 Med W/T Sec R Sigs PWB
Air O P Party RAF NIKSIC
(1) General Morale of all units is of a high standard, and the more active conditions are the more it improves.
(2) The only noticeable grumble about conditions was due to delays in the forwarding of mail from Italy. It is hoped this matter is now adjusted.

(3) The opening of the leave scheme LIAP has provided a topic of conversation, though mathematicians are not wanting who point out that a rate of approx 10% per annum is rather a "lucky" dip. It has undoubtedly cheered up all ranks who feel it is a good beginning and will be improved upon.

(4) The main difficulty with the FORCE has been the impact upon them of the governmental methods of the Partisan authorities. All units had experience of the JANL in VIS and had a great admiration for them and their discipline, cleanliness, and honesty. Conditions in the Yugoslavia mainland have definitely shocked them, and indeed been at times most irksome. This is especially true in DUBROVNIK and TREBINJE, the only large towns in which troops have had to be stationed. The lack of freedom to express an opinion or to speak to civilians: the harsh treatment of GERMAN prisoners: the summary executions of Yugoslavs who have been Chetniks, or are suspected of Chetnik, i. e. non-Partisan, sympathies: have all tended to make the situation tense.
Lectures and explanations have been given, and orders have been issued on these matters explaining that the country is both fighting the GERMANS and running an internal revolution at the same time.

(5) The troops have, on the whole, behaved very well and have understood the necessity for restraint. Nevertheless they have acquired a deep-rooted dislike for the TITO system, and the general tone is that "it’s not that sort of thing we're fighting for".
(6) Morale would rapidly deteriorate were troops not afforded the opportunity of action, in which they bear up with great spirit under arduous and very uncomfortable conditions.
a) Union Jack newspapers do not come through with rations. If these could be arranged an issue of more than the normal 10% would be 'necessary owing to the large area over which the force is spread.

(b) Pew Wireless Sets are at present held within this FORCE and it is considered that at the present stage of the war that men are greatly interested in the news. A supply of Wireless Sets in addition to, or in lieu of, newspapers would be beneficial to morale.
